publications.title: SELECT-HF: Semaglutide in Patients With Heart Failure With Preserved Ejection Fraction — Phase 3 Randomized Trial
publications.summary: BACKGROUND: Obesity and type 2 diabetes are common in heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) and associate with worse outcomes. Semaglutide, a GLP-1 receptor agonist, improves weight and glycemic control in this population. METHODS: We randomized adults with HFpEF (LVEF ≥45%), NYHA class II–IV symptoms, and either BMI ≥30 kg/m² or BMI ≥27 kg/m² with controlled type 2 diabetes to once-weekly subcutaneous semaglutide 2.4 mg or placebo. Primary endpoint was time to composite of cardiovascular death or worsening heart failure events at 52 weeks. RESULTS: Semaglutide reduced the primary endpoint versus placebo (hazard ratio 0.79; 95% CI 0.64–0.98; P=0.03), driven mainly by fewer heart failure hospitalizations. KCCQ clinical summary score improved more with semaglutide. Body weight and NT-proBNP decreased. Gastrointestinal adverse events were more frequent with semaglutide; serious hypoglycemia was rare. CONCLUSIONS: In HFpEF with obesity-related risk, semaglutide improved heart failure outcomes and quality-of-life measures, supporting GLP-1 class evaluation in cardiometabolic HFpEF.

publications.title: Inclisiran for LDL Cholesterol Reduction in Patients With Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease or High Risk: Pooled Safety and Efficacy
publications.summary: IMPORTANCE: Sustained LDL-C lowering with infrequent dosing may improve adherence in secondary prevention. OBJECTIVE: To characterize LDL-C reductions and cardiovascular event rates with twice-yearly inclisiran (siRNA targeting PCSK9) versus placebo in addition to statin therapy. DESIGN: Pooled analysis of phase 3 ORION trials in patients with ASCVD or equivalent risk. INTERVENTION: Inclisiran 300 mg subcutaneous at day 1, day 90, then every 6 months. MAIN OUTCOMES: Percent change in LDL-C at 18 months and exploratory MACE components. RESULTS: Mean LDL-C decreased by approximately 50% from baseline with inclisiran versus modest change with placebo; reductions were durable between doses. Injection-site reactions were the most common adverse event; hepatic enzyme elevations were infrequent and balanced. Exploratory analyses suggested directional benefit on major adverse cardiovascular events versus placebo in long-term follow-up extensions. CONCLUSIONS: Inclisiran offers potent, sustained LDL lowering with a twice-yearly schedule relevant to long-term cardiovascular risk management in statin-treated patients.

publications.title: Apixaban Versus Warfarin for Stroke Prevention in Atrial Fibrillation With One Additional Risk Factor: Real-World Effectiveness Cohort
publications.summary: BACKGROUND: Non-vitamin K oral anticoagulants (NOACs) are preferred when appropriate, but comparative effectiveness in broader community cohorts remains important for payer and hospital formulary decisions. METHODS: Retrospective cohort study of adults with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation initiating apixaban or warfarin, with CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 and no prior OAC exposure. Primary outcome: composite of ischemic stroke, systemic embolism, or intracranial hemorrhage. Secondary outcomes included major bleeding and all-cause mortality. RESULTS: Apixaban was associated with lower rates of the primary composite and intracranial hemorrhage versus warfarin after propensity matching. Major non-intracranial bleeding rates were similar. All-cause mortality favored apixaban in the matched cohort. LIMITATIONS: Residual confounding despite matching; claims-based outcomes. CONCLUSIONS: In a real-world population, apixaban showed favorable effectiveness and safety relative to warfarin, consistent with randomized trial directions and supporting NOAC adoption in stroke prevention for atrial fibrillation.

publications.title: SGLT2 Inhibitors and Kidney Outcomes in Type 2 Diabetes With Albuminuria: Meta-Analysis of Cardiovascular and Renal Trials
publications.summary: RATIONALE: Sodium–glucose cotransporter-2 (SGLT2) inhibitors reduce heart failure hospitalizations and slow chronic kidney disease progression, but magnitude across albuminuria subgroups is debated. OBJECTIVE: Quantify effects on composite kidney endpoints (≥40% sustained eGFR decline, end-stage kidney disease, or renal death) and cardiovascular death in patients with type 2 diabetes stratified by UACR. DATA SOURCES: Randomized placebo-controlled trials reporting urinary albumin-to-creatinine ratio at baseline. ANALYSIS: Random-effects models; heterogeneity assessed with I². RESULTS: SGLT2 inhibitors reduced kidney composite endpoints consistently across normo-, micro-, and macroalbuminuria strata. Cardiovascular death reduction was most pronounced in macroalbuminuria. Acute kidney injury signals were balanced by longer-term protection; ketoacidosis risk was low but higher with SGLT2 class. CONCLUSIONS: SGLT2 inhibitors provide cardiorenal protection in T2D across albuminuria levels; macroalbuminuric patients show the largest absolute risk reduction, informing portfolio prioritization for renal-cardiovascular comorbidity.

publications.title: Ivabradine in Chronic Stable Coronary Disease Without Heart Failure: Symptom Burden and Exercise Capacity
publications.summary: BACKGROUND: Ivabradine lowers heart rate by selective If channel inhibition and is approved in selected heart failure populations; its role in chronic angina without reduced ejection fraction is less uniform across guidelines. METHODS: Multicenter randomized double-blind trial of ivabradine versus placebo on top of beta-blocker and antianginal therapy in chronic stable angina with documented coronary disease, sinus rhythm, and resting heart rate ≥70 bpm. Co-primary endpoints: change in angina frequency class and total exercise duration on treadmill at 16 weeks. RESULTS: Ivabradine reduced resting and exercise heart rate versus placebo. Angina class improved in a greater proportion of ivabradine-treated patients; exercise time increased modestly but significantly. Visual analog scale angina scores improved. Bradycardia and luminous phenomena were more common with ivabradine. CONCLUSIONS: In selected chronic angina patients with inadequate symptom control, ivabradine offers incremental anti-ischemic benefit and supports heart-rate–guided management alongside traditional antianginals.

publications.title: Erenumab for Episodic Migraine Prevention: Long-Term Open-Label Extension and Discontinuation Effects
publications.summary: BACKGROUND: CGRP receptor antagonism reduces migraine days in episodic migraine; durability and relapse after withdrawal inform lifecycle planning. METHODS: Participants completing prior erenumab trials entered a 52-week open-label extension receiving monthly 70 or 140 mg. Primary focus: long-term safety, migraine-day reduction, and acute medication use. A structured withdrawal substudy assessed headache frequency 12 weeks after last dose. RESULTS: Migraine monthly headache days remained below baseline through the extension; adverse events were consistent with prior trials (injection-site reactions, constipation). In the withdrawal cohort, migraine days increased toward baseline but generally remained below pre-treatment levels at 12 weeks off therapy, suggesting partial sustained benefit or selection effects. CONCLUSIONS: Erenumab maintains efficacy over 12 months with a tolerable profile; post-discontinuation migraine frequency increases but may not immediately return to pretreatment burden—relevant for payer coverage of anti-CGRP prophylaxis and R&D on cycling strategies.

publications.title: Duloxetine for Painful Diabetic Peripheral Neuropathy: Patient-Reported Outcomes and Sleep Quality
publications.summary: OBJECTIVE: Evaluate whether duloxetine improves pain intensity, sleep, and function versus placebo in painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy (PDPN) over 12 weeks. DESIGN: Randomized double-blind parallel-group trial; primary endpoint mean change in 24-hour average pain severity (11-point NRS). Secondary endpoints included Brief Pain Inventory interference, Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index, and Patient Global Impression of Change. RESULTS: Duloxetine 60 mg once daily separated from placebo on pain NRS at weeks 4–12; 120 mg daily showed incremental benefit with higher discontinuation due to nausea. Sleep quality subscores improved in parallel with pain. Small mean changes in blood pressure were monitored; no new unexpected safety signals in the PDPN population. CONCLUSIONS: Duloxetine provides clinically meaningful pain and sleep-related quality-of-life improvements in PDPN, supporting serotonin–norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors as a pharmacologic pillar for neuropathic pain portfolios.

publications.title: Tanezumab in Moderate-to-Severe Osteoarthritis of the Hip or Knee: Efficacy and Joint Safety Signals
publications.summary: BACKGROUND: Nerve growth factor (NGF) inhibitors are potent analgesics for osteoarthritis (OA); joint safety (rapid OA progression, subchondral insufficiency fracture) has influenced regulatory decisions. METHODS: Phase 3 program comparing subcutaneous tanezumab versus NSAID and/or opioid comparators in moderate-to-severe OA pain inadequately controlled with standard care. Primary endpoint: Western Ontario and McMaster Universities Osteoarthritis Index (WOMAC) pain subscale change at 24 weeks. RESULTS: Tanezumab demonstrated superior pain reduction versus active comparators in pooled analyses; physical function improved. Incidence of rapidly progressive OA and need for total joint replacement was higher in tanezumab arms versus comparators in certain subgroups. Peripheral neuropathy symptoms were monitored. CONCLUSIONS: Tanezumab offers substantial OA pain relief but carries distinctive musculoskeletal safety considerations that must shape risk management programs, patient selection, and competitive positioning versus conventional analgesics and intra-articular therapies.

publications.title: Buprenorphine Transdermal System Versus Tapentadol Extended-Release for Chronic Low Back Pain: A Randomized Noninferiority Study
publications.summary: PURPOSE: Compare opioid-sparing transdermal buprenorphine to tapentadol ER for chronic noncancer low back pain with inadequate response to nonopioid analgesics. METHODS: 22-week randomized open-label with blinded outcome assessment; primary endpoint change in average pain intensity (NRS). Secondary endpoints included Oswestry Disability Index, rescue medication use, and treatment satisfaction. RESULTS: Buprenorphine patch titration achieved noninferior pain reduction versus tapentadol; constipation rates favored buprenorphine in exploratory comparisons. Withdrawals for lack of efficacy were balanced; dizziness occurred in both arms. CONCLUSIONS: Transdermal buprenorphine provides an alternative oral-sparing option for selected chronic low back pain patients, with implications for abuse-deterrent portfolio design and pain-management guidelines in musculoskeletal disease.

publications.title: Canagliflozin After Myocardial Infarction in Patients With Type 2 Diabetes: Post-Hoc Analysis of Cardiovascular and Heart Failure Events
publications.summary: BACKGROUND: SGLT2 inhibitors reduce hospitalization for heart failure in T2D with cardiovascular risk; post–myocardial infarction (MI) subgroups are clinically salient for secondary prevention messaging. METHODS: Post-hoc analysis of a cardiovascular outcomes trial in T2D, focusing on participants with prior MI at baseline. Endpoints included three-point MACE, hospitalization for heart failure, and progression of albuminuria. RESULTS: Canagliflozin versus placebo showed consistent reductions in HF hospitalization in the post-MI stratum; MACE point estimates trended favorably without definitive statistical separation in the subset. Renal composite endpoints favored canagliflozin. Hypovolemia and genital mycotic infections occurred more frequently with active treatment. LIMITATIONS: Subgroup analyses are hypothesis-generating. CONCLUSIONS: Post-MI patients with T2D may derive particular heart failure–related benefit from SGLT2 inhibition, informing integrated cardiovascular–metabolic pipeline narratives and competitive differentiation in high-risk post-acute coronary syndromes.
